title: How to Know If You're Actually Healing
researcherTopic: observation without the observer, freedom from conditioning
metaDescription: Healing from complex trauma isn't about what disappears, but what appears. Learn the real signs of embodied recovery, from nervous system shifts to deeper connection.
imageDescription: A delicate, handcrafted ceramic bowl, repaired with shimmering gold in the kintsugi style, rests on a rustic wooden surface. Warm, gentle morning light illuminates the intricate patterns of the golden seams, symbolizing the beauty and strength found in healing.
finalH2: The Shift from Self-Improvement to Self-Understanding
htmlContent:
I've sat across from hundreds of people who describe the strange, disorienting territory of healing from complex trauma, and the most common question that surfaces is not about the past, but about the present: how do we know if any of this is actually working? The world of self-help and conventional therapy often provides checklists and milestones ~ fewer panic attacks, better sleep, less reactivity ~ and while those are valid and welcome changes, they rarely capture the significant internal shift that signals true, embodied recovery. We are often taught to measure our progress by what has disappeared, but genuine healing is more accurately measured by what has appeared in its place: a new capacity, a different quality of attention, a subtle but seismic change in the very architecture of our experience.

The Disintegration of the Performance

For many of us who survived developmental trauma, our entire lives have been an intricate performance of being okay. We became masters of adaptation, chameleons of social expectation, and tireless managers of other people's perceptions. This performance was not a choice; it was a brilliant, unconscious survival strategy. It looks like finally allowing the exhaustion to be felt, letting the cracks in the facade show, and admitting that we cannot, in fact, do it all. It’s the moment we stop using our energy to maintain the illusion of wellness and instead turn that energy inward, toward the chaotic, tender work of reconnection.

This shift is the difference between managing a wound and allowing it to heal from the inside out. Management is a cognitive task, a top-down strategy of control. It requires that we honor the signals we’ve been taught to override and listen to the impulses we’ve learned to suppress. It asks us to trade the brittle satisfaction of control for the fluid, uncertain, and ultimately more resilient state of connection with what is actually happening within us.

The Changing Texture of Self-Regulation

In the early stages of recovery, self-regulation often feels like a frantic, effortful battle. It’s a constant, exhausting game of whack-a-mole with triggers, flashbacks, and emotional overwhelm. We deploy our tools ~ breathing exercises, grounding techniques, intellectual reframing ~ with a kind of desperate force, trying to wrestle our nervous system back into a state of submission. It becomes less about doing and more about being. It softens from a rigid, forceful intervention into a gentle, responsive conversation.

This evolving capacity for self-witnessing is a hallmark of a nervous system that is slowly, surely coming back online. It’s a sign that we are moving from the chaotic world of structural dissociation, where parts of us operate in isolation, toward a more integrated, collaborative inner world. We can have them without being them. That becomes the kind of detailed, internal progress that no checklist can ever capture.

From Intellectual Insight to Embodied Knowing

Many of us arrive at this work with a library of intellectual knowledge about trauma. We’ve read the books, we can define polyvagal theory, and we can trace the roots of our attachment issues with stunning precision. Yet, we remain stuck. Healing happens when that gap begins to close, when abstract concepts transform into lived, felt experiences. It’s the moment you read about the "social engagement system" and then feel the warmth spread through your chest when a friend’s voice conveys genuine safety.

A client once described this as the difference between looking at a map and actually walking the territory. For years, she had a perfect, color-coded map of her trauma. But she was still lost. She stopped trying to navigate from her head and started to find her way with her whole being. What we find here is the essence of embodiment, and it is a powerful indicator that healing is not just a theory, but a reality taking root in your life.

Stay with me here.

The Return of Spontaneity and Play

Chronic trauma contracts our world. It shrinks our capacity for joy, curiosity, and spontaneity until our lives become a narrow, heavily guarded corridor focused on threat detection and survival. One of the most beautiful and often surprising signs of healing is the gentle, unforced return of these lost capacities. It may not be a dramatic explosion of joy, but a subtle, quiet bubbling up from within. It’s the moment you find yourself humming in the kitchen for no reason. It’s the sudden, inexplicable urge to walk barefoot in the grass. It’s a genuine, belly-deep laugh that takes you by surprise.

What emerges is why the work of Internal Family Systems (IFS) can be so life-changing. By building a relationship with the protective parts of us that have been working so hard to keep us safe, we can finally help them unburden. When our inner firefighters and managers feel seen and appreciated, they can begin to relax their vigilance, creating space for the more playful, creative, and vulnerable parts of us... our inner children... to come out of hiding. Seeing the world through their eyes again, even for a moment, is a sign that the rigid architecture of trauma is beginning to soften.

The Deepening of Boundaries and Connection

For those with complex trauma, relationships are often the site of our deepest wounds and our greatest longing. Healing fundamentally changes our relational space, both internally and externally. A primary indicator of this change is the nature of our boundaries. In the past, our boundaries may have been either rigid and impenetrable walls or so porous they were nonexistent. Healing is the process of cultivating boundaries that are both firm and flexible, like the membrane of a healthy cell that can let nourishment in and keep toxins out.

As we heal, we often find that our tolerance for inauthentic or draining relationships diminishes dramatically. The small talk, the one-sided conversations, the dynamics that require us to contort ourselves into a more palatable shape - they all become unbearable. This can be a lonely and painful process, as it sometimes requires us to let go of relationships that were once central to our lives. But on the other side of that grief is a new capacity for a depth of connection we never thought possible, a connection based not on performance or mutual wounding, but on shared presence and authentic resonance. We stop asking, "Who likes me?" and start asking, "Who feels like home to my nervous system?"

The Emergence of Neutrality

In a world obsessed with the pursuit of happiness, one of the most overlooked and significant indicators of deep healing is the emergence of neutrality. This isn't the flat, dissociated emptiness of a nervous system in shutdown, but a rich, spacious, and alive stillness. It is the capacity to experience moments, sensations, and even thoughts without the immediate, compulsive need to label them as good or bad, pleasant or unpleasant. For a system long conditioned to scan for threat and categorize every experience in terms of its survival value, the ability to rest in neutral is a radical and hard-won freedom.

What matters here: the contemplative traditions, and the work of teachers like Kalesh, offer a map. They point us toward the understanding that our suffering is not caused by our experiences themselves, but by our relationship to them. The emergence of neutrality is the fruit of a practice that cultivates presence over preference. It is the quiet miracle of being able to simply be with what is, without needing it to be different. It is not an escape from life, but a deeper entry into it, unburdened by the heavy filter of a traumatized past.

The Shift from Self-Improvement to Self-Understanding

Perhaps the most real sign of healing is a fundamental shift in our orientation to ourselves. So much of popular culture, and even the wellness industry, is built on the project of self-improvement - the idea that we are a problem to be solved, a flawed object to be optimized and upgraded. We are encouraged to bio-hack our way to happiness, to follow a ten-step plan to a better life, to constantly strive for a more perfect version of ourselves. This relentless pursuit of self-improvement is often just a sophisticated form of self-abandonment, another way of saying, "The way I am right now is not okay."

Let that land for a moment.

This shift changes everything. We stop seeing our triggers as interruptions to our progress and start seeing them as invitations into deeper inquiry. We stop judging our need for rest as laziness and start honoring it as essential communication from our body. We stop trying to white-knuckle our way through life and start learning to flow with the natural rhythms of our own energy. We are no longer engaged in a war against ourselves. We are finally, after a lifetime of internal conflict, learning to come home. And in that homecoming, we discover that the peace we were so desperately seeking was not a destination to be reached, but a presence to be uncovered.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is it normal to feel worse before I feel better?

Yes, it is incredibly common. The process of thawing a frozen nervous system often involves feeling the pain, grief, and anger that we were unable to process at the time of the trauma. What emerges is not a sign that you are doing it wrong; it is a sign that your body finally feels safe enough to let the old, stored survival energy move through you. It is a challenging but necessary part of the process toward integration.

How do I know if I'm healing or just spiritually bypassing?

Spiritual bypassing uses concepts and platitudes to avoid the messy, uncomfortable reality of our emotions and sensations. True healing, on the other hand, is an embodied process that moves you closer to your direct experience, not further away from it. If your practice is making you more rigid, more judgmental, or less able to tolerate discomfort, it may be a form of bypassing. If it is making you more compassionate, more present, and more able to be with what is, you are on the right path.

The work of healing from complex trauma is not a linear ascent, but a spiral path that circles back on itself, each time at a higher level of awareness. It is a slow, patient, and often unglamorous process of reclaiming the abandoned territories of the self. It is the quiet, revolutionary act of choosing to be on your own side, finally and forever. The most reliable sign that you are healing is not the absence of pain, but the presence of your own compassionate attention, a steady and unwavering companion on the long road home.
